title: Hydroxyapatite/metal composite coatings formed by electrocodeposition
abstract:
Hydroxyapatite/metal composite coatings formed 
by electrocodeposition 
Harini Dasarathy,' Clyde Riley,'.* H. Dwain Coble,' William R. Lacefield: and George Maybee3 
'Department of Chemistry and Materials Science, University of Alabama in Huntsville, Huntsville, Alabama 35899; 
2Department of Biomaterials, UAB, SDB Box 49, Birmingham, Alabama 35294-1240; 3McDonnell Douglas Corporation, 
Huntsville, Alabama 35806 
Early bone infusion by cementless fixation of composite or- 
thopedic and dental implants consisting of metallic sub- 
strates and bioceramics is well documented. Calcium phos- 
phate ceramics in general and hydroxyapatite (HA) in 
particular have been the most popular of the bioceramics 
used for coating metals. Here, a non-line of sight coating 
procedure by electrocodeposition is reported for mechani- 
cally fixing HA particles in a metal matrix. 
Analyses of the coating showed excellent adhesion to the 
substrate and no structural transformation in either crystal- 
linity or stoichiometry. Adhesion and surface coverage of 
HA depended upon the particle size. As a demonstration of 
the coating procedure's non-line of sight applicability, it was 
successfully used to coat titanium rods sintered with small 
titanium spheres. 0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
INTRODUCTION 
Two widely used orthopedic and dental implant ma- 
terials are Ti-A1-V alloy and Co-Cr-Mo alloy. In the 
past 3 decades, research in this field has been oriented 
toward coating these metal implant materials with 
bioceramics to reduce the implant failures due to isola- 
tion and to obtain cementless fixation of the implant. 
Calcium phosphate ceramics (CPC) have been the most 
popular of bioceramics because of their apparent pro- 
pensity to accelerate bone growth around an implant. 
Calcium phosphate occurs in several different phases 
that are collectively called CPCs.' One of the crystalline 
phases of CPC has the formula Ca10(P04)6(OH)2 
and is 
known as hydroxyapatite (HA). Tooth enamel is 
mainly HA, while bones contain up to 60% of this 
compound.2 With the exception of small structures of 
hard tissue associated with the ear, all normal hard 
tissues and many pathologic calcifications in the ani- 
mals are apatiti~.~ 
It was logical that an implant coated 
with this material would possibly enhance bone forma- 
tion around the implant and contribute to cementless 
fi~ation.~-~ 
It has since been shown to enhance bone 
growth by bonding with bone ti~sue.~ 
Accordingly, it 
is categorized as both an osteointegrating and osteo- 
conductive ceramic.* HA found in bone is microcrystal- 
line, contains carbonate, is calcium deficient, and is 
* To whom correspondence should be addressed. 
Journal of Biomedical Materials Research, Vol. 31, 81-89 (1996) 
0 1996 John Wiley 8.1 Sons, Inc. 
structurally disordered, while synthetic HA prepared 
at high temperatures is carbonate free and is more 
perfectly crystalline.' 
Many coating procedures have been documented, 
but all have shortcomings.'0 The most widely applied 
coating procedure today is the plasma spray coating 
method. HA subjected to the extreme high tempera- 
tures of a plasma flame results in structural modifica- 
tions of the applied ceramic coating and is restricted 
to line of sight  application^."-'^ 
In this article we report application of an established 
technique to give a new method for coating HA on 
metal substrates. This development, which was the 
subject of a recent patent, involves electrocodeposition 
of HA in a metal matrix, which is a new approach 
for biomaterial fabri~ation.'~ 
The coated surface was 
subsequently characterized for morphology and com- 
position using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) 
and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Structure 
was determined by Fourier transform infrared spec- 
troscopy (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopy, and coating 
adhesion to the substrate was determined by tensile 
pull testing. 
Electrocodeposition for HA coating 
Electrocodeposition is a process by which a metal 
can be deposited in concert with another metal (metals 
in solutions as positive ions), nonmetal, or ceramic 
CCC 0021-9304/96/ 010081 -09 
 82 
DASARATHY ET AL. 
(physically suspended in medium) onto a substrate 
(cathode) from an electrolytic bath. Figure 1 shows a 
schematic representation of an experimental setup to 
perform such a process. Electrocodeposition of inert 
particles in a metal matrix is one of numerous methods 
of making cermet code posit^.'^^^^ Cermet codeposits 
prepared in this manner might include particles for 
increasing hardness, e.g., diamond or silicon carbide 
in nickel, molybdenum sulfide in chromium and chro- 
mium carbide, or tungsten carbide in cobalt. Thus, the 
presence of these codeposited particles (mechanically 
attached by inclusion in the metal matrix) in the surface 
modifies the surface properties. Since the inclusion of 
HA particles on the surface of a metallic implant makes 
it possible to combine the bioactivity of HA with the 
mechanical properties of the underlying substrate, 
thereby increasing the life span of the implant in the 
host as a result of enhanced bone infusion and bonding, 
we proposed using an electrodeposition process to 
bond unaltered crystalline HA strongly and mechani- 
cally to the implant-type materials. 
Theory of codeposition 
The detailed mechanism of codeposition is still an 
unresolved issue, although various mechanisms have 
been suggested. The two models which presently fit 
experimental data best and are applied most often are 
those formulated by Gugliemi19 and Celis et a1.2" 
Parameters affecting electrocodeposition 
Agitation and particle size 
Homogeneous or uniform inclusion of inert particles 
by electrocodeposition requires uniform suspension of 
the particles in the bath throughout the plating period. 
Particle inclusion depends upon the agitation rate until 
a maximum suspension pattern is achieved.20 The rate 
1
I
 
In1 
++, 
METER B- 
AMMETER 
Figure 1. Schematic representation of the experimental 
setup. 
of agitation should be such that there is a constant 
availability of the particles near the cathode, but the 
particles should be retained long enough at the cathode 
to facilitate their anchoring by the depositing metal 
atoms. Inadequate stirring results in particle sedimen- 
tation, while vigorous stirring removes the adsorbed 
particle from the cathode even before enough metal 
ions are reduced to ensure inclusion. 
Particle concentration 
The effect of particle concentration on particle inclu- 
sion has been documented by Zahavi and Hazan." 
They argued that inclusion in the deposit increases as 
the concentration of particles in the bath increases, 
until a critical concentration level is reached. It then 
falls off because of particle congestion, which reduces 
the number of particles reaching the cathode. This ex- 
planation holds well for particle sizes of 7 pm or larger, 
but for smaller-sized particles (of the order of 3 pm) 
the particle inclusion depends linearly on the particle 
concentration of the bath. 
Current density 
It is generally observed that lower current density 
deposits result in a higher percentage of particle inclu- 
sion. This behavior depends upon whether the parti- 
cles are conducting or nonconducting. Conducting par- 
ticle inclusion is favored by lower current densities 
(1 mA/cmZ), while the nonconducting particles favor 
higher deposition rates (up to 35 mA/cm2). In the case 
of conducting particles, lower current densities and 
smaller particle sizes (3-6 pm) yield uniform inclusion 
with smooth surfaces. For nonconducting particles, 
uniformity of the inclusion is not affected by higher 
current densities. 
Additives 
Bath constituents affect the codeposition in many 
ways. Addition of some of the antipitting agents leaves 
the deposit smoother, while addition of surfactants 
may increase or decrease the particle inclusion. The 
nature of the surfactant (anionic, cationic, or ampho- 
teric) is the deciding factor, since the best results are 
obtained if the particles do not have any charge on 
them. Other additives can be brighteners such as boric 
acid or buffers to maintain a constant pH throughout 
the deposition period. 
PH 
pH does not show a large effect on codeposition. 
However, care should be taken to ensure that the solu- 
tion operational pH range does not adversely affect 
the particles physically or chemically.

Electrocodeposition for HA coating
Electrocodeposition is a process by which a metal
can be deposited in concert with another metal (metals
in solutions as positive ions), nonmetal, or ceramic
(physically suspended in medium) onto a substrate
(cathode) from an electrolytic bath. Figure 1 shows a
schematic representation of an experimental setup to
perform such a process. Electrocodeposition of inert
particles in a metal matrix is one of numerous methods
of making cermet code posit^.'^^^^ Cermet codeposits
prepared in this manner might include particles for
increasing hardness, e.g., diamond or silicon carbide
in nickel, molybdenum sulfide in chromium and chro-
mium carbide, or tungsten carbide in cobalt. Thus, the
presence of these codeposited particles (mechanically
attached by inclusion in the metal matrix) in the surface
modifies the surface properties. Since the inclusion of
HA particles on the surface of a metallic implant makes
it possible to combine the bioactivity of HA with the
mechanical properties of the underlying substrate,
thereby increasing the life span of the implant in the
host as a result of enhanced bone infusion and bonding,
we proposed using an electrodeposition process to
bond unaltered crystalline HA strongly and mechani-
cally to the implant-type materials.
The detailed mechanism of codeposition is still an
unresolved issue, although various mechanisms have
been suggested. The two models which presently fit
experimental data best and are applied most often are
those formulated by Gugliemi19 and Celis et a1.2"

Homogeneous or uniform inclusion of inert particles
by electrocodeposition requires uniform suspension of
the particles in the bath throughout the plating period.

a maximum suspension pattern is achieved.20 The rate

pH does not show a large effect on codeposition.
However, care should be taken to ensure that the solu-
tion operational pH range does not adversely affect
the particles physically or chemically.
The temperature of the plating solution does not
directly affect codeposition. A higher temperature
could increase or decrease the plating rate of the metal,
which in turn might increase or decrease the percent-
age of particles in the deposit.
Several platings were carried out, changing plating
parameters such as current density and anode cathode
distance to arrive at standardized conditions for the
HA codeposition process. Cobalt was chosen as the
depositing metal matrix for the demonstration, because
most of the earlier codeposition work in the literature
was with this metal. Cobalt is also known to give good
contact with inert particles such as tungsten carbide.
In addition, this metal is a component of F75, composed
of cobalt, chromium, and molybdenum in the ratio
The thickness of the metal deposit assuming 100%
current efficiency was calculated by rearranging Fara-
where T = thickness of the deposit in micrometers;
I = current density in amperes per square centimeter;
M = gram atomic weight of the metal in grams per
mole; n = number of electrons in the reduction; F =
Faraday constant; d = density of the metal in grams
per cubic centimeter; and t = time in seconds. The sub-
strates for all depositions were 0.36-cm-thick, commer-
cially pure titanium discs 0.62 cm in diameter. They

to assist in both suspending the sample and making
an electrical connection. A constant distance of 1 cm
was maintained between the anode and cathode for
all runs. Care was taken to ensure that the substrate
was not located in any vortex created by stirring, which
was maintained at 180 rpm. This rate of stirring did
not usually form a vortex in the bath, but was sufficient
to maintain a suspension and keep the particles from

Plating conditions were: DC plating time, gener-

Post-treatment of the coated discs consisted of wash-
ing the deionized water and acetone. Examination of
morphology was by SEM, and analyses for composi-
tion and structure were performed with EDS, IT-IR,
and Raman spectroscopy. The IT-IR was a Nicolet 740
with IR plan microscope, and the Raman was a 3A
Jobin Yvon UlOOO with a laser excitor. Adhesion
strength between the coating and the substrate was
determined using a Sebastian five bond tester by Quad
Group. The SEM used was a JSM-8220 by JEOL with
a scanning range of X12-300,000, and which was
equipped with an X-ray detector by Kevex Co (Model
2003). Kevex Quantex software was used for energy-
dispersive microanalysis, which is a comprehensive
package of software tooled for acquisition and process-
ing of X-rays generated by an electron column. It runs
on a Kevex Microanalyst 7500,8000, or Delta-class ana-
Initially, we established a baseline maximum bond
strength between cobalt and the titanium substrate. A
set of four titanium discs were coated with a uniform
cobalt coating at 10 mA/cm2 for 15 min for this test.
To determine whether deposition of HA and metal
would work, we then prepared an exploratory set of
10 HA/cobalt composite coatings on titanium. For this
study, we synthesized the crystalline HA in our labora-
tory and limited the HA particle size to < 35 pm by
passing the crystals through screens. Pretreatments
and electrodepositions were as delineated previously.
Using information from this exploratory work, we pro-
ceeded to finetune the coating process.
Optimal HA particle size determination
Inert particle size in a codeposition process greatly
influences the volume percent of particles in the de-
posit.22 The adhesion of the particles to the substrate
is also a function of the surface area to volume ratio.
The percent inclusion of particles is dependent upon
the conductivity of the inert particles. Thus, it was
necessary to determine the particle size that provided
the best coverage and adhesion.
Hydroxyapatite was commercially available in vari-
ous sizes from 10-100 pm. Its electrical resistivity is
of the order of 4 X lo9 R cm and is thus categorized
as a nonconducting material. This is consistent with
the observation that the particles show charging under
electron probing. To determine the relationship be-
tween particle size and the percentage of HA in a
codeposited surface, the adhesion study was carried
out using various particle sizes to determine the opti-
mal HA particle size for coating by electrodeposition.
Three size brackets of HA particles were used to
perform the study: 10-20, 20-40, and 40-80 pm. For
this determination, HA was obtained in these three
size groups from Osteonics Inc. (NJ). Preliminary mi-
croscopic examination of the three sizes revealed there
was considerable overlap between 10-20- and 20-
40-pm sizes. The 40-80-pm group showed some parti-
cles of < 10 pm occluded on the surface of larger parti-
cles. All three sizes were codeposited under standard-
ized conditions delineated earlier, except for time. The
deposition time was different for each size depending
upon the thickness of the coating necessary to embed
50% of the average size particle into the matrix [deter-
mined by Eq. (111. After post-treatment, the samples
were stored in such a way that deposited surfaces were
completely free of contact from any foreign material.
To accumulate statistical data, a set of 15 coatings was
used for each of the three sizes. All 45 samples were
examined under SEM and micrographs were obtained
for each sample. To maintain consistency for later anal-
ysis, all the micrographs were obtained with the same
accelerating voltage (10 KV), working distance (8 mm),
and magnification (X370). The micrographs were ob-
tained from the center region of samples, which was
also the region used for collecting coating adhesion
data as described in the following section. These micro-
graphs were used to estimate the percentage coverage
of HA on the surface by image analysis. EDS data for
a few representative samples in each of the three sizes
were also obtained. The coatings were then tested for
adhesion with the same bond tester machine used for
earlier recordings to facilitate data comparison. The
sample preparation for adhesion testing was identical
for all the coatings. The fractured area was examined
for some of these samples by SEM.
Bond testing is instrumentation and procedure-
dependent. For the Sebastian Five, the procedure in-
volved fastening a 3.6 X 12-mm pull stud coated with
a thermal curing epoxy to the center of the coated
surface, which was then cured at 150°C for 1 h. At this
time, the epoxy melted completely before polymeriza-
tion and assured that the stud was perpendicular to
the coated surface. Establishing perpendicularity was
important for sample comparison, because nonperpen-
dicular fastening would apply the stress at an angle
other than 90", leading to erroneous values for the bond
strength. The machine was activated when the stud
was pulled down against the support ridge until the
failure occurred in the coating. The bond strength of
a fully cured epoxy was 70 MPa (-10 ksi), and thus
was the upper limit of bond strength that might be
determined with this apparatus.
To explore the viability of the electrodeposition pro-
cess for substrates of different geometries, especially
those not practical for the plasma spray method, a
highly irregularly shaped substrate with many small
titanium beads sintered onto a stainless-steel rod was
coated uisng the standardized conditions described in
the previous section. The coated substrate was then
cross-sectioned and examined under SEM.
Image analysis for HA coverage estimation
Hydroxyapatite-codeposited surfaces under elec-
tron probing with an SEM showed two distinctive fea-
tures or regions. These are conductive metal matrix
areas and nonconductive areas of HA. Because of the
nonconductive nature of HA, these particles hold
charge and their image becomes bright, while the con-
ducting metal regions remain relatively darker. This
contrast was translated into a micrograph and used to
calculate the percentage of bright and dark areas which
is related to the HA and metal percentages on the
surface. A measurement of these areas necessitated a
detailed image analysis of the micrographs.
Micrographs were first scanned using a Microtek
MSF-300GS image scanner under control of a Macin-
tosh system. The scanning was done using Digital
Darkroom software. The resolution used for the scans
was 300 dots dpi, and a 256-level gray-scale scanning
mode was employed. After several trial iterations, opti-
mum brightness and contrast settings were identified
for all micrographs. The micrographs' images were
analyzed using IMAGE software from The National
Institutes of Health. With this software it was possible
to identify the gray-level of any point on the image.
This facilitated selection of an optimum threshold
point within the histogram representing the crossover
point between the HA and metal matrix. The frequency
distribution of the histogram was then used to calculate
the percentages of pixels below and above the thresh-
old level which corresponded to the extent of HA and
Codeposited HA characterization
Since calcium phosphate ceramics can occur in sev-
eral different phases, it is important to be able to iden-
tify their presence in a substrate’s coating. Transmis-
sion IR has been a choice method to determine phase
changes, but transmission IR requires removal of the
coating from any opaque substrate to collect the IR
spectra for characterization, and thus is a destructive
In earlier work, we combined spectroscopy and mi-
croscopy to make IR characterization a nondestructive
technique for HA surface analysis by collecting the
spectra while retaining the integrity of the substrate
coating. In addition, another method of characterizing
HA deposits, Raman spectroscopy, was used. The com-
plimentary data from the two techniques were then
analyzed to determine the HA phase transformations.
It was found that a combination of reflectance infrared
and Raman spectroscopy could be used to make an in
situ characterization of the calcium phosphate phases,
A plot of calculated (using Faraday’s equation) co-
balt deposit thickness in microns versus the time of
plating for a fixed current density of 10 mA/cmZ is
shown in Figure 2. SEM examination of electrodepos-
ited neat cobalt showed a leaflike structure, which is
typical for an uninterrupted deposit of a metal with a
hexagonal close-packed structure. Coating adhesion
data for neat cobalt on titanium gave an average of
50.6 (?5.5) MPa. The adhesion value for neat cobalt
on titanium would be the average upper limit that
could be expected for a cobalt-HA coating on titanium.
The average bond strength for the 10 cobalt-HA coat-
Figure 3. Micrograph of the codeposited cobalt/HA surface
ings in the preliminary study (Ha prepared in the labo-
ratory) was 10.7 (55.4) MPa. While this bond strength
was significantly lower than for neat cobalt deposition,
it revealed that the process was viable for HA codeposi-
tion but needed further refinement.
The micrograph of an HA-codeposited sample (syn-
thesized in our laboratory) is shown in Figure 3. This
shows that both particle size and shape varied consid-
erably, and since particle size affects adhesion, it was
probably the reason for their low bond strengths. SEM
examination of the coated surfaces prepared with com-
mercial HA using three different particle size ranges
of 10-20,20-40, and 40-80 pm showed a fairly uniform
distribution of particles for all three. However, it was
evident that particle shape in all three sizes was uni-
form. As expected, because of particle size overlap, the
average bond strengths (15 samples in each size range)
for 10-20- and 20-40-pm-size coatings were very close,

sities demonstrating when HA was considered present.

with 37.4 (516.6) and 35.6 (k12.8) MPa, respectively.
These values were much higher compared to the pre-
liminary set of samples prepared using in-house syn-
thesized HA because of better uniformity in shape and
size distribution within the size range. The bond
strength for the 40-80-pm-size coating averaged much
The EDS result gave the elemental composition of a
codeposited sample. The absence of a titanium peak
showed that the surface was totally covered by the
codeposit. EDS results for codeposited samples for all
three sizes (the individual HA particles could be seen
embedded in the codeposited cobalt metal matrix) con-
sistently showed peaks representing Ca, P, and Co
A micrograph of the cross-section of the coating on
the titanium-sintered bead specimen is shown in Fig-
ure 4. This micrograph shows that codeposition oc-
curred not only on the surface of the titanium beads
but also in the crevices between the beads. Such a
codeposition was possible because the particles were
carried even to non-line of sight surfaces of the sam-
ple with the stirred electrolyte during the codeposi-
tion. This confirmed that the process is applicable to
substrates of any geometry and that it can overcome
a major limitation of both plasma-sprayed and ion-
To determine surface coverage of the HA in the coat-
ings using image analysis, the frequency distribution
Figure 6. Coverage of HA on the surface vs. average particle
in terms of the number of pixels (picture elements) of
the various gray-level intensities within the image
were charted as a histogram shown in Figure 5. Our
best estimates of the HA coverage on the surface ob-
tained by image analysis of the micrographs are given
in Figure 6. The data indicate that all three sizes had
an average of > 50% coverage of HA on the surface.
Some samples gave as high as 80% coverage. This result
showed that particle size was not a critical criterion
for the high surface coverage of HA obtained under
the conditions used in these experiments.
Table I lists the IR and Raman absorption frequencies
for Poi3 and OH-. The spectrum obtained from dif-
fused reflectance by FT-IR spectroscopy of 5% HA
powder in KBr is shown in Figure 7 and corresponds
well with that reported by others.'2J3 The same sample
when scanned in the reflectance mode through the
microscope at X200 has a different appearance; the
spectrum is shown in Figure 8. Figure 9(a and b) are
the Raman spectra for the same sample. Details for
application of reflectance FT-IR and surface Raman
spectroscopies for the in situ characterization of CPC
coatings can be found elsewhere."
Figure 10 is a typical IR spectrum for the surface
coating prepared with HA particle sizes of 10-20 pm.
The microscope enables one to focus and reflect the IR
from a small region of particles. The corresponding
Raman spectra for the composite are shown in Figure
ll(a and b). A comparison of Figures 7 and 8 reveals
that the use of a microscope increases the noise level
in the spectrum. The C-0 stretch of carbon dioxide
IR and Raman Absorption Frequencies for PO, and OH Groups
Figure 7. FTIR spectrum (DRIFT) of 5% starting HA powder
(present as a surface impurity) was more pronounced
when data were collected through the microscope.
Most of the spectra reported here were obtained only
Examination of the spectrum for the starting HA
powder using the microscope (Fig. 8) shows that all
absorptions were discernible for this method of data
collection. The Raman probe-derived spectra of Figure
9a and b further resolve the peaks of interest. The
extent of the clarity of these peaks was evident in that
the degeneracy of each peak was apparent. Although
the absorption peaks were better resolved in the Raman
compared to the IR spectrum, more comprehensive
results can be obtained using both techniques, espe-
Figure 8. FTIR spectrum of starting HA powder through
cially when monitoring and comparing coatings with
varied thickness or ones that include composite matrix
material. While IR data are useful in thin coatings,
Raman data are better as a monitor when particles are
surrounded by other material such as a metal matrix,
because the focus is essentially on individual particles.
The IR spectrum of the composite coating depicted
in Figure 10, when compared with the spectrum of the
starting powder (Fig. 8), showed absorptions at the
same frequencies. The differences seen in the 1200 and
2000 cm-' regions resulted from the low reflectance
in the starting powder and higher reflectance in the
composite surface, because of the presence of the sec-
ond material in the composite. This was concluded
after studying many composite samples differing from
each other only in the percentage of surface covered

absorptions from 431-1075 cm-'. fb) Raman spectrum of

Figure 10. FTIR spectrum of composite coating showing
by HA particles. As the percentage of HA coverage
decreased, increased reflectivity and scattering due to
the second material resulted in a reduced peak defini-
tion. A comparison of the Raman spectra [Fig. ll(a
and b)l with their counterparts for the starting powder
[Fig. 9(a and b)] gave peak matching without any in-
crease in the noise level resulting from the second ma-
terial of the composite. From these spectra it could be
deduced that HA on these surfaces had not been al-
tered from the starting material.
Electrocodeposition can be successfully employed to
cover a metal substrate (titanium) with a composite
coating of HA embedded in a cobalt matrix. This was
confirmed by the EDS data which showed Co, Ca, P,
and 0 with the exclusion of Ti. Further, when EDS
data were collected from an HA particle on the surface,
it showed only Ca, P, and 0 with no cobalt, which
indicated that there was no reaction between the parti-
Hydroxyaptite particles of various sizes could be
used, but those above 80 pm become difficult to sus-
pend for electrodeposition. The size of the particles
was found to be a significant factor in determining
the adhesion obtained between the coating and the
substrate. Particle size increases led to a decrease in ad-
Particle size and particle geometry altered the homo-
geneity of the coverage of HA on the surface. Smaller
particles gave more uniform coverage. The most uni-
form coverage was obtained with particles in the
Figure 11. (a) Raman spectrum of composite coating show-
ing P a 3  absorptions demonstrating no change from starting
HA. (b) Raman spectrum of composite coating showing OH-
absorption demonstrating no change from starting HA.
FT-IR and Raman spectra indicated characteristic
peak matches between coated HA and neat HA. This
confirmed that the specific conditions used in the elec-
trocodeposition did not alter the structure of HA.
The process is a non-line of sight application, indi-
cating its suitability for coating porous surfaces.
While cobalt alone is not a satisfactory metal matrix
for implants, the results demonstrate the viability of the
technique. Using a biocompatible metal-alloy matrix
deposited by electrodeposition for anchoring HA may
result in a superior bonded coating of unaltered crys-
talline HA that could be used on implants.
